instruction: Walk past the kitchen and go behind the couch and take a right into the fitness room. Stop next to the treadmill.

text: I'll start by observing the current state and looking around to get my bearings.

I think I made a navigation error—I ended up in the kitchen instead of heading toward the couch. Let me reconsider my turn direction and the coordinate system. The relative directions are labeled as ahead (0°), right (+90°), behind (+180°), and left (+270°), but I need to verify how these map to my actual heading angle.

Looking back at my movements: I turned right 90 degrees and moved forward, which actually put me in the "right" view direction rather than the "left" direction where the couch was. To correct this, I should backtrack by turning 180 degrees to face the way I came, move forward 6 steps to return to the junction, then turn left 90 degrees to face the couch area properly.

Let me clarify the heading system: if the look_around function rotates clockwise through the views (ahead, right, behind, left), then "left" corresponds to a 270-degree clockwise rotation from the original heading, which is equivalent to 90 degrees counter-clockwise.

So I need to turn left 90 degrees to face the sitting area, not right. My earlier right turn took me to the kitchen instead. To correct this, I'll turn around 180 degrees (either direction works) to reverse my path back to the junction, then move forward. After that 180-degree turn, my heading will already be θ0+90, which is the "left" facing direction—so I can just move forward from the junction into the couch area without needing an additional turn.
